edges.description: Loss of CYB5R3 activity impairs reduction of ferric methemoglobin back to oxygen-binding hemoglobin.
Generalized CYB5R3 deficiency across all tissues in type II disease causes neurological involvement beyond the erythrocyte-restricted form.
Inefficient reduction causes chronic ferric methemoglobin accumulation.
Ferric methemoglobin cannot bind oxygen efficiently, causing cyanosis and exertional symptoms.

nodes.description: Ascorbic acid has been reported as symptomatic treatment for cyanosis in recessive congenital methemoglobinemia.

evidence.reference_title: A rare cause of mental motor retardation: recessive congenital methemoglobinemia type II.
evidence.snippet: Cyanosis can be well treated by 200-500 mg of ascorbic acid daily
CYB5R3 | cytochrome b5 reductase 3 | hgnc:2873 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Recessive congenital methaemoglobinaemia: cytochrome b(5) reductase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: It is encoded by the CYB5R3 (previously known as DIA1) gene and more than 40 mutations have been described

evidence.reference_title: Mutation update: Variants of the CYB5R3 gene in recessive congenital methemoglobinemia.
evidence.snippet: over 78 different variants have been described for RCM globally.

nodes.description: Biallelic CYB5R3 pathogenic variants reduce NADH-cytochrome b5 reductase activity, the erythrocyte enzyme system that normally transfers electrons needed to reduce ferric methemoglobin.

evidence.reference_title: Recessive congenital methaemoglobinaemia: cytochrome b(5) reductase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: This disorder, now known as recessive congenital methaemoglobinaemia (RCM), is caused by NADH-cytochrome b5 reductase (cb(5)r) deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation update: Variants of the CYB5R3 gene in recessive congenital methemoglobinemia.
evidence.snippet: NADH-cytochrome b5 reductase 3 deficiency is an important genetic cause of recessive congenital methemoglobinemia (RCM)

molecular_functions: cytochrome-b5 reductase activity, acting on NAD(P)H

nodes.description: Cyanosis is typically present from birth or infancy.

evidence.reference_title: Recessive congenital methaemoglobinaemia: cytochrome b(5) reductase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: both characterized by cyanosis from birth.

evidence.reference_title: Enzymopenic Congenital Methemoglobinemia in Children of the Republic of Sakha (Yakutia).
evidence.snippet: physical examination revealed cyanosis of the skin and mucus membranes.

nodes.description: Methaemoglobin is continuously formed as heme iron becomes ferric; CYB5R3 deficiency prevents efficient reduction back to the ferrous state.

evidence.snippet: the rate at which ferric heme is formed and the rate at which it is reduced back to the ferrous state.
the methaemoglobin formed at physiological rate is not efficiently reduced.

nodes.description: Inefficient reduction of ferric methemoglobin leads to chronically elevated methemoglobin levels in blood.

evidence.snippet: A chronically elevated level of oxidized haemoglobin results.

evidence.reference_title: Enzymopenic Congenital Methemoglobinemia in Children of the Republic of Sakha (Yakutia).
evidence.snippet: The median methemoglobin level was 13.5% (ranging between 4.2% and 33.9%)

nodes.description: Methylene blue can lower methemoglobin levels and is generally reserved for symptomatic or significantly elevated methemoglobinemia; evidence in type II congenital disease is limited to case-level symptomatic benefit.

evidence.snippet: treatment with methylene blue is reserved for patients with significantly elevated methemoglobin levels.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Methemoglobinemia Type II-Clinical Improvement with Short-Term Methylene Blue Treatment.
evidence.snippet: modest behavioral improvements (as assessed on the Achenbach behavior report scales).

nodes.description: Methemoglobin is unable to bind oxygen, so chronic methemoglobin elevation reduces effective oxygen transport and produces cyanosis, hypoxia, and dyspnea.

evidence.snippet: Methaemoglobin is incapable of binding oxygen.
The diagnosis of methemoglobinemia should be considered in patients presenting with cyanosis and hypoxia.

nodes.description: Severe type II disease reflects more generalized CYB5R3 dysfunction, especially variants affecting FAD-binding or active-site function, and is associated with neurodevelopmental impairment and movement disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation update: Variants of the CYB5R3 gene in recessive congenital methemoglobinemia.
evidence.snippet: The majority of the mutations associated with the severe form with a neurological disorder (RCM Type 2) were associated with the FAD-binding domain

evidence.reference_title: Recessive congenital methemoglobinemia type II: Hypoplastic basal ganglia in two siblings with a novel mutation of the cytochrome b5 reductase gene.
evidence.snippet: mild neonatal cyanosis, early onset severe progressive developmental delay, movement disorders, and progressive microcephaly.
